--- title: "Unit 7: Linear & Quadratic Functions" number-sections: true number-depth: 2 execute: echo: false --- ## 🎯 Learning Targets ::: lt-grid ::: lt-card ::: {.lt-badge .c1} LT1 ::: ::: lt-title tables]{.lt-skill .c1} [Arrow diagrams]{.lt-skill .c1} [Vertical line test]{.lt-skill .c1} ::: ::: ::: lt-card ::: {.lt-badge .c2} LT2 ::: ::: lt-title Representations ::: ::: lt-desc Move fluently between tables, graphs, and equations for linear functions. ::: ::: lt-skills [Table to graph]{.lt-skill .c2} [Graph to equation]{.lt-skill .c2} [Multiple representations]{.lt-skill .c2} ::: ::: ::: lt-card ::: {.lt-badge .c3} LT3 ::: ::: lt-title Domain & Range ::: ::: lt-desc Identify the domain and range of a relation from a set, table, or graph. ::: ::: lt-skills [Input & output]{.lt-skill functions in real-world contexts. ::: ::: lt-skills [Evaluate $f(x)$]{.lt-skill .c4} [Identify linear]{.lt-skill .c4} [Interpret context]{.lt-skill .c4} ::: ::: ::: lt-card ::: {.lt-badge .c5} LT5 ::: ::: lt-title Slope & Rate of Change ::: ::: lt-desc Calculate slope from two points or a table and interpret it as a rate of change in context. ::: ::: lt-skills [Slope formula]{.lt-skill .c5} [From a table]{.lt-skill .c5} [Rate of change]{.lt-skill .c5} ::: ::: ::: lt-card ::: {.lt-badge .c1} LT6 ::: ::: lt-title Linear Equations & Graphs ::: ::: lt-desc Write and graph linear equations; find equations of parallel and perpendicular lines. ::: ::: lt-skills [Slope-intercept]{.lt-skill .c1} [Parallel lines]{.lt-skill .c1} [Perpendicular lines]{.lt-skill .c1} ::: ::: ::: lt-card ::: {.lt-badge .c2} LT7 ::: ::: lt-title Linear Modelling ::: ::: lt-desc Form, solve, and interpret linear models for real-world situations. ::: ::: lt-skills [Form the model]{.lt-skill .c2} [Solve & predict]{.lt-skill .c2} [Interpret results]{.lt-skill .c2} ::: ::: ::: lt-card ::: {.lt-badge .c3} LT8 ::: ::: lt-title Quadratic Functions ::: ::: lt-desc Identify quadratic functions, evaluate them, and convert between vertex, standard, and factored forms. ::: ::: lt-skills [Vertex form]{.lt-skill .c3} [Standard form]{.lt-skill .c3} [Factored form]{.lt-skill .c3} ::: ::: ::: lt-card ::: {.lt-badge .c4} LT9 ::: ::: lt-title Key Features of Graphs ::: ::: lt-desc Determine the vertex, roots, and axis of symmetry of a parabola from its equation or graph. ::: ::: lt-skills [Vertex]{.lt-skill .c4} [Roots / x-intercepts]{.lt-skill .c4} [Axis of symmetry]{.lt-skill .c4} ::: ::: ::: lt-card ::: {.lt-badge .c5} LT12 ::: ::: lt-title Domain & Range — Quadratic ::: ::: lt-desc Determine and interpret the domain and range of a quadratic function from its equation or graph. ::: ::: lt-skills [Domain: all reals]{.lt-skill .c5} [Range from vertex]{.lt-skill .c5} [Opens up or down]{.lt-skill .c5} ::: ::: ::: lt-card ::: {.lt-badge .c1} LT13 ::: ::: lt-title Quadratic Modelling ::: ::: lt-desc Model and interpret real-world situations using quadratic functions, including projectile motion and revenue problems. ::: ::: lt-skills [Projectile motion]{.lt-skill .c1} [Revenue problems]{.lt-skill .c1} [Interpret vertex]{.lt-skill .c1} ::: ::: ::: ## 🗂️ Formula Card :::::::::::: formula-card ### Linear & Quadratic Functions — Key Facts {.unnumbered} #### Relations & Functions {.unnumbered} ::: sticky-note-blue **💡 Key Test** A relation is a **function** if every input ($x$-value) maps to **exactly one** output ($y$-value). Use the vertical line test on a graph. ::: ::: rule-group **Function notation**: $f(x) = mx + c$ means "the output when the input is $x$" **Domain**: the set of all valid **inputs** ($x$-values) **Range**: the set of all valid **outputs** ($y$-values) **Examples**: $$f(x) = 2x + 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad f(3) = 2(3)+1 = 7$$ ::: #### Linear Functions {.unnumbered} ::: sticky-note-yellow **📝 Slope Formula** $$m = \frac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$$ ::: ::: rule-group **Slope-intercept form**: $y = mx + c$, where $m$ = slope, $c$ = $y$-intercept **Parallel lines** share the **same slope**: $m_1 = m_2$ **Perpendicular lines**: slopes are **negative reciprocals**: $m_1 \times m_2 = -1$ **Examples**: $$\text{Through }(2,5)\text{ with slope }3: \quad y - 5 = 3(x-2) \quad \Rightarrow \quad y = 3x - 1$$ $$\text{Perpendicular to }y=2x+1: \quad m_\perp = -\tfrac{1}{2}$$ ::: #### Quadratic Functions — Three Forms {.unnumbered} ::: sticky-note-pink **⚠️ Conversion Tip** Each form reveals different information. Convert using expansion or completing the square. ::: ::: rule-group | Form | Equation | Reveals | |---|---|---| | **Standard** | $f(x) = ax^2 + bx + c$ | $y$-intercept $(0, c)$ | | **Vertex** | $f(x) = a(x-h)^2 + k$ | Vertex $(h, k)$ | | **Factored** | $f(x) = a(x-r_1)(x-r_2)$ | Roots $x = r_1, r_2$ | **Vertex formula** (from standard form): $$x_{\text{vertex}} = \frac{-b}{2a}$$ **Axis of symmetry**: $x = \dfrac{-b}{2a} = h$ ::: #### Converting Between Forms {.unnumbered} ::: rule-group **Vertex → Standard** (expand): $$2(x-3)^2 + 1 = 2(x^2-6x+9)+1 = 2x^2 - 12x + 19$$ **Vertex → Factored** (find roots first): $$\textstyle(x-3)^2 - 4 = 0 \Rightarrow x = 3\pm2 \Rightarrow f(x)=(x-1)(x-5)$$ **Standard → Vertex** (complete the square): $$x^2 - 6x + 5 = (x-3)^2 - 9 + 5 = (x-3)^2 - 4$$ **Factored → Standard** (expand): $$(x-1)(x-5) = x^2 - 6x + 5$$ ::: #### Domain & Range {.unnumbered} ::: sticky-note-orange **🎯 Quick Rule** Every quadratic has domain $x \in \mathbb{R}$. The range depends on whether the parabola opens up or down. ::: ::: rule-group | Function type | Domain | Range | |---|---|---| | Linear $f(x) = mx+c$ | $x \in \mathbb{R}$ | $y \in \mathbb{R}$ | | Quadratic, $a > 0$ (opens up) | $x \in \mathbb{R}$ | $y \geq k$ | | Quadratic, $a < 0$ (opens down) | $x \in \mathbb{R}$ | $y \leq k$ | ::: :::::::::::: ## 📝 Practice Problems :::::::::: panel-tabset ### 🟢 Easy ::::: {#exr-u7-1} *Analyse the arrow diagram.* The arrow diagram shows a relation. **(a)** State the domain and range of this relation. **(b)** Is this relation a function? Justify your answer. ::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"} ## 🔍 View Solution $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Check: does each domain element have exactly one arrow?} \\[4pt] \text{No — one input maps to more than one output.} \end{aligned} $$ ::: ::: exercise-meta
Function definition Domain & range Arrow diagrams
::: ::::: ::::: {#exr-u7-2} *Analyse the relation.* Consider the relation: $\{\, (2,\ -1), (3,\ 4), (-4,\ -1), (5,\ -3), (5,\ -2) \,\}$ **(a)** State the domain and range. **(b)** Is this a function? Explain. ::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"} ## 🔍 View Solution $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Domain: } \{-4, 2, 3, 5\} \\[4pt] \text{Range: } \{-3, -2, -1, 4\} \\[4pt] \text{Function? No — one $x$-value maps to two outputs.} \end{aligned} $$ ::: ::: exercise-meta
Function definition Domain & range Arrow diagrams
::: ::::: ::::: {#exr-u7-3} *Find the equation of the linear function shown in the table.* :::: {.grid} ::: {.g-col-12} **a)** $\displaystyle \begin{array}{c|cccc} x & -2 & -1 & 3 & 4 \\ \hline y & 3 & 0 & -12 & -15 \end{array}$ ::: ::: {.g-col-12} **b)** $\displaystyle \begin{array}{c|cccc} x & -3 & -2 & 0 & 3 \\ \hline y & -3 & -2 & 0 & 3 \end{array}$ ::: ::: {.g-col-12} **c)** $\displaystyle \begin{array}{c|cccc} x & -3 & 0 & 1 & 3 \\ \hline y & -9 & 0 & 3 & 9 \end{array}$ ::: ::: {.g-col-12} **d)** $\displaystyle \begin{array}{c|cccc} x & -3 & 0 & 2 & 3 \\ \hline y & -5 & 4 & 10 & 13 \end{array}$ ::: :::: ::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"} ## 🔍 View Solutions **a)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Slope: } m = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \dfrac{-3}{1} = -3 \\[4pt] \text{Substitute } (-2, 3): 3 = -3(-2) + c \\[4pt] c = -3 \\[4pt] y = -3x - 3 \end{aligned} $$ **b)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Slope: } m = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \dfrac{1}{1} = 1 \\[4pt] \text{Substitute } (-3, -3): -3 = 1(-3) + c \\[4pt] c = 0 \\[4pt] y = x \end{aligned} $$ **c)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Slope: } m = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \dfrac{9}{3} = 3 \\[4pt] \text{Substitute } (-3, -9): -9 = 3(-3) + c \\[4pt] c = 0 \\[4pt] y = 3x \end{aligned} $$ **d)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Slope: } m = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \dfrac{9}{3} = 3 \\[4pt] \text{Substitute } (-3, -5): -5 = 3(-3) + c \\[4pt] c = 4 \\[4pt] y = 3x + 4 \end{aligned} $$ ::: ::: exercise-meta
Table of values Linear equations Representations
::: ::::: ::::: {#exr-u7-4} *Complete the table of values.* :::: {.grid} ::: {.g-col-12} **a)** $\displaystyle y = -2x + 2 \qquad \begin{array}{c|cccc} x & -1 & 0 & 1 & 4 \\ \hline y & \square & 2 & 0 & \square \end{array}$ ::: ::: {.g-col-12} **b)** $\displaystyle y = -x \qquad \begin{array}{c|cccc} x & -2 & -1 & 3 & 4 \\ \hline y & 2 & 1 & \square & \square \end{array}$ ::: ::: {.g-col-12} **c)** $\displaystyle y = 2x + 2 \qquad \begin{array}{c|cccc} x & -1 & 0 & 2 & 3 \\ \hline y & 0 & \square & 6 & \square \end{array}$ ::: ::: {.g-col-12} **d)** $\displaystyle y = -x - 4 \qquad \begin{array}{c|cccc} x & 0 & 1 & 3 & 4 \\ \hline y & -4 & \square & \square & -8 \end{array}$ ::: :::: ::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"} ## 🔍 View Solutions **a)** $$ \begin{aligned} x = -1: \quad y = -2(-1) + 2 = 4 \\[4pt] x = 4: \quad y = -2(4) + 2 = -6 \end{aligned} $$ **b)** $$ \begin{aligned} x = 3: \quad y = -1(3) + 0 = -3 \\[4pt] x = 4: \quad y = -1(4) + 0 = -4 \end{aligned} $$ **c)** $$ \begin{aligned} x = 0: \quad y = 2(0) + 2 = 2 \\[4pt] x = 3: \quad y = 2(3) + 2 = 8 \end{aligned} $$ **d)** $$ \begin{aligned} x = 1: \quad y = -1(1) - 4 = -5 \\[4pt] x = 3: \quad y = -1(3) - 4 = -7 \end{aligned} $$ ::: ::: exercise-meta
Table of values Linear equations Representations
::: ::::: ::::: {#exr-u7-5} *State the domain and range of each relation.* :::: {.grid} ::: {.g-col-12} **a)** $\displaystyle \{\,(1,\ -1), (3,\ 2), (0,\ -2), (-1,\ 4), (4,\ 0)\,\}$ ::: ::: {.g-col-12} **b)** $\displaystyle \{\,(2,\ 2), (1,\ -1), (-2,\ 4), (3,\ -3), (-4,\ -3)\,\}$ ::: ::: {.g-col-12} **c)** $\displaystyle \{\,(2,\ -3), (3,\ 4), (0,\ 4), (-4,\ -3), (4,\ 0)\,\}$ ::: ::: {.g-col-12} **d)** $\displaystyle \{\,(1,\ -3), (0,\ 2), (-2,\ 3), (-4,\ 0), (4,\ -2)\,\}$ ::: :::: ::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"} ## 🔍 View Solutions **a)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Domain (all }x\text{-values): } \{-1, 0, 1, 3, 4\} \\[4pt] \text{Range (all }y\text{-values): } \{-2, -1, 0, 2, 4\} \end{aligned} $$ **b)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Domain (all }x\text{-values): } \{-4, -2, 1, 2, 3\} \\[4pt] \text{Range (all }y\text{-values): } \{-3, -1, 2, 4\} \end{aligned} $$ **c)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Domain (all }x\text{-values): } \{-4, 0, 2, 3, 4\} \\[4pt] \text{Range (all }y\text{-values): } \{-3, 0, 4\} \end{aligned} $$ **d)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Domain (all }x\text{-values): } \{-4, -2, 0, 1, 4\} \\[4pt] \text{Range (all }y\text{-values): } \{-3, -2, 0, 2, 3\} \end{aligned} $$ ::: ::: exercise-meta
Domain Range Set notation
::: ::::: ::::: {#exr-u7-6} *State the domain and range of each function.* :::: {.grid} ::: {.g-col-3} **a)** $\displaystyle f(x) = 3x - 1$ ::: ::: {.g-col-3} **b)** $\displaystyle f(x) = 3x + 3$ ::: ::: {.g-col-3} **c)** $\displaystyle f(x) = 2x - 2$ ::: ::: {.g-col-3} **d)** $\displaystyle f(x) = -2x$ ::: :::: ::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"} ## 🔍 View Solutions **a)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Linear functions are defined for all real numbers.} \\[4pt] \text{Domain: } x \in \mathbb{R} \\[4pt] \text{Range: } y \in \mathbb{R} \end{aligned} $$ **b)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Linear functions are defined for all real numbers.} \\[4pt] \text{Domain: } x \in \mathbb{R} \\[4pt] \text{Range: } y \in \mathbb{R} \end{aligned} $$ **c)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Linear functions are defined for all real numbers.} \\[4pt] \text{Domain: } x \in \mathbb{R} \\[4pt] \text{Range: } y \in \mathbb{R} \end{aligned} $$ **d)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Linear functions are defined for all real numbers.} \\[4pt] \text{Domain: } x \in \mathbb{R} \\[4pt] \text{Range: } y \in \mathbb{R} \end{aligned} $$ ::: ::: exercise-meta
Domain Range Set notation
::: ::::: ::::: {#exr-u7-7} *Identify which expression represents a linear function.* :::: {.grid} ::: {.g-col-12} **a)** $\displaystyle \text{A:}\; |t| + 1 \qquad \text{B:}\; \dfrac{1}{t} + 4 \qquad \text{C:}\; 2t^2 + t + 1 \qquad \text{D:}\; -2t + 3$ ::: ::: {.g-col-12} **b)** $\displaystyle \text{A:}\; \dfrac{1}{y} + 1 \qquad \text{B:}\; |y| + 5 \qquad \text{C:}\; -2y - 5 \qquad \text{D:}\; 2y^2 - 3y + 3$ ::: ::: {.g-col-12} **c)** $\displaystyle \text{A:}\; 2n - 3 \qquad \text{B:}\; 3n^2 - 2n + 4 \qquad \text{C:}\; \dfrac{1}{n} + 2 \qquad \text{D:}\; |n| + 3$ ::: ::: {.g-col-12} **d)** $\displaystyle \text{A:}\; 2m^2 - 2m - 1 \qquad \text{B:}\; |m| + 3 \qquad \text{C:}\; \dfrac{1}{m} + 1 \qquad \text{D:}\; m + 3$ ::: :::: ::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"} ## 🔍 View Solutions **a)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{A linear function has degree 1 (form } f(x) = mx + c\text{).} \\[4pt] \text{Answer: } D \text{ — } f(x) = -2t + 3 \text{ has degree 1. }\checkmark \end{aligned} $$ **b)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{A linear function has degree 1 (form } f(x) = mx + c\text{).} \\[4pt] \text{Answer: } C \text{ — } f(x) = -2y - 5 \text{ has degree 1. }\checkmark \end{aligned} $$ **c)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{A linear function has degree 1 (form } f(x) = mx + c\text{).} \\[4pt] \text{Answer: } A \text{ — } f(x) = 2n - 3 \text{ has degree 1. }\checkmark \end{aligned} $$ **d)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{A linear function has degree 1 (form } f(x) = mx + c\text{).} \\[4pt] \text{Answer: } D \text{ — } f(x) = m + 3 \text{ has degree 1. }\checkmark \end{aligned} $$ ::: ::: exercise-meta
Linear functions Slope y-intercept
::: ::::: ::::: {#exr-u7-8} *Evaluate the function at each given value.* :::: {.grid} ::: {.g-col-12} **a)** $\displaystyle f(x) = 2x - 3 \quad \text{Find: } f(-3), f(4), f(2)$ ::: ::: {.g-col-12} **b)** $\displaystyle f(x) = 3x - 6 \quad \text{Find: } f(0), f(-3), f(3)$ ::: ::: {.g-col-12} **c)** $\displaystyle f(x) = -x - 5 \quad \text{Find: } f(0), f(-1), f(2)$ ::: ::: {.g-col-12} **d)** $\displaystyle f(x) = x - 3 \quad \text{Find: } f(-1), f(2), f(1)$ ::: :::: ::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"} ## 🔍 View Solutions **a)** $$ \begin{aligned} f(-3) = 2 \cdot (-3) - 3 = -9 \\[4pt] f(4) = 2 \cdot (4) - 3 = 5 \\[4pt] f(2) = 2 \cdot (2) - 3 = 1 \end{aligned} $$ **b)** $$ \begin{aligned} f(0) = 3 \cdot (0) - 6 = -6 \\[4pt] f(-3) = 3 \cdot (-3) - 6 = -15 \\[4pt] f(3) = 3 \cdot (3) - 6 = 3 \end{aligned} $$ **c)** $$ \begin{aligned} f(0) = -1 \cdot (0) - 5 = -5 \\[4pt] f(-1) = -1 \cdot (-1) - 5 = -4 \\[4pt] f(2) = -1 \cdot (2) - 5 = -7 \end{aligned} $$ **d)** $$ \begin{aligned} f(-1) = 1 \cdot (-1) - 3 = -4 \\[4pt] f(2) = 1 \cdot (2) - 3 = -1 \\[4pt] f(1) = 1 \cdot (1) - 3 = -2 \end{aligned} $$ ::: ::: exercise-meta
Linear functions Slope y-intercept
::: ::::: ::::: {#exr-u7-10} *Find the slope of the line through each pair of points.* :::: {.grid} ::: {.g-col-4} **a)** $\displaystyle (3,\ 2) \text{ and } (1,\ -1)$ ::: ::: {.g-col-4} **b)** $\displaystyle (-3,\ 2) \text{ and } (-4,\ 5)$ ::: ::: {.g-col-4} **c)** $\displaystyle (2,\ 3) \text{ and } (6,\ -1)$ ::: ::: {.g-col-4} **d)** $\displaystyle (-3,\ -4) \text{ and } (-2,\ -2)$ ::: ::: {.g-col-4} **e)** $\displaystyle (-3,\ -1) \text{ and } (-5,\ -2)$ ::: ::: {.g-col-4} **f)** $\displaystyle (3,\ -2) \text{ and } (4,\ -4)$ ::: :::: ::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"} ## 🔍 View Solutions **a)** $$ \begin{aligned} m = \dfrac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} = \dfrac{-1 - (2)}{1 - (3)} = \dfrac{-3}{-2} = \dfrac{3}{2} \end{aligned} $$ **b)** $$ \begin{aligned} m = \dfrac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} = \dfrac{5 - (2)}{-4 - (-3)} = \dfrac{3}{-1} = -3 \end{aligned} $$ **c)** $$ \begin{aligned} m = \dfrac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} = \dfrac{-1 - (3)}{6 - (2)} = \dfrac{-4}{4} = -1 \end{aligned} $$ **d)** $$ \begin{aligned} m = \dfrac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} = \dfrac{-2 - (-4)}{-2 - (-3)} = \dfrac{2}{1} = 2 \end{aligned} $$ **e)** $$ \begin{aligned} m = \dfrac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} = \dfrac{-2 - (-1)}{-5 - (-3)} = \dfrac{-1}{-2} = \dfrac{1}{2} \end{aligned} $$ **f)** $$ \begin{aligned} m = \dfrac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} = \dfrac{-4 - (-2)}{4 - (3)} = \dfrac{-2}{1} = -2 \end{aligned} $$ ::: ::: exercise-meta
Rate of change Slope formula Interpretation
::: ::::: ::::: {#exr-u7-11} *Find the rate of change from the table.* :::: {.grid} ::: {.g-col-12} **a)** $\displaystyle \begin{array}{c|cccc} x & -2 & 1 & 4 & 5 \\ \hline y & -1 & 5 & 11 & 13 \end{array}$ ::: ::: {.g-col-12} **b)** $\displaystyle \begin{array}{c|cccc} x & -2 & -1 & 3 & 5 \\ \hline y & 0 & -2 & -10 & -14 \end{array}$ ::: ::: {.g-col-12} **c)** $\displaystyle \begin{array}{c|cccc} x & -2 & 0 & 1 & 4 \\ \hline y & 4 & 2 & 1 & -2 \end{array}$ ::: ::: {.g-col-12} **d)** $\displaystyle \begin{array}{c|cccc} x & -1 & 0 & 4 & 5 \\ \hline y & 0 & 2 & 10 & 12 \end{array}$ ::: :::: ::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"} ## 🔍 View Solutions **a)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Rate of change} = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \dfrac{6}{3} = 2 \\[4pt] \text{Constant rate → linear, slope } m = 2 \end{aligned} $$ **b)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Rate of change} = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \dfrac{-2}{1} = -2 \\[4pt] \text{Constant rate → linear, slope } m = -2 \end{aligned} $$ **c)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Rate of change} = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \dfrac{-2}{2} = -1 \\[4pt] \text{Constant rate → linear, slope } m = -1 \end{aligned} $$ **d)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Rate of change} = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \dfrac{2}{1} = 2 \\[4pt] \text{Constant rate → linear, slope } m = 2 \end{aligned} $$ ::: ::: exercise-meta
Rate of change Slope formula Interpretation
::: ::::: ::::: {#exr-u7-18} *Identify which expression represents a quadratic function.* :::: {.grid} ::: {.g-col-12} **a)** $\displaystyle \text{A:}\; 3t^2 - 3t - 3 \qquad \text{B:}\; 3t^3 - 1t^2 + 3 \qquad \text{C:}\; 2 \qquad \text{D:}\; -2t + 4$ ::: ::: {.g-col-12} **b)** $\displaystyle \text{A:}\; 2p^3 + 2p^2 + 3 \qquad \text{B:}\; -p^2 + 3p + 4 \qquad \text{C:}\; 2 \qquad \text{D:}\; -p + 4$ ::: ::: {.g-col-12} **c)** $\displaystyle \text{A:}\; 3n^3 - 1n^2 + 3 \qquad \text{B:}\; -2n^2 + 4n - 2 \qquad \text{C:}\; -n - 1 \qquad \text{D:}\; 9$ ::: ::: {.g-col-12} **d)** $\displaystyle \text{A:}\; -3x + 1 \qquad \text{B:}\; -2x^2 + 2x \qquad \text{C:}\; 2x^3 x^2 + 2 \qquad \text{D:}\; 8$ ::: :::: ::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"} ## 🔍 View Solutions **a)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{A quadratic has highest degree 2.} \\[4pt] \text{Answer: } A \text{ — } f(x) = 3t^2 - 3t - 3 \text{ has degree 2. }\checkmark \end{aligned} $$ **b)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{A quadratic has highest degree 2.} \\[4pt] \text{Answer: } B \text{ — } f(x) = -p^2 + 3p + 4 \text{ has degree 2. }\checkmark \end{aligned} $$ **c)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{A quadratic has highest degree 2.} \\[4pt] \text{Answer: } B \text{ — } f(x) = -2n^2 + 4n - 2 \text{ has degree 2. }\checkmark \end{aligned} $$ **d)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{A quadratic has highest degree 2.} \\[4pt] \text{Answer: } B \text{ — } f(x) = -2x^2 + 2x \text{ has degree 2. }\checkmark \end{aligned} $$ ::: ::: exercise-meta
Quadratic functions Form conversions Vertex form
::: ::::: ::::: {#exr-u7-19} *Evaluate each quadratic function at the given values.* :::: {.grid} ::: {.g-col-12} **a)** $\displaystyle f(t) = -2t^2 - 3t - 2 \quad \text{Find: } f(-2), f(0), f(-1)$ ::: ::: {.g-col-12} **b)** $\displaystyle f(n) = 2n^2 - 2n - 4 \quad \text{Find: } f(0), f(-2), f(3)$ ::: ::: {.g-col-12} **c)** $\displaystyle f(n) = -n^2 - n - 3 \quad \text{Find: } f(2), f(1), f(-1)$ ::: ::: {.g-col-12} **d)** $\displaystyle f(m) = -m^2 - 2m - 2 \quad \text{Find: } f(-2), f(-1), f(0)$ ::: :::: ::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"} ## 🔍 View Solutions **a)** $$ \begin{aligned} f(-2) = -2(-2)^2 + 6 - 2 = -4 \\[4pt] f(0) = -2(0)^2 + 0 - 2 = -2 \\[4pt] f(-1) = -2(-1)^2 + 3 - 2 = -1 \end{aligned} $$ **b)** $$ \begin{aligned} f(0) = 2(0)^2 + 0 - 4 = -4 \\[4pt] f(-2) = 2(-2)^2 + 4 - 4 = 8 \\[4pt] f(3) = 2(3)^2 - 6 - 4 = 8 \end{aligned} $$ **c)** $$ \begin{aligned} f(2) = -1(2)^2 - 2 - 3 = -9 \\[4pt] f(1) = -1(1)^2 - 1 - 3 = -5 \\[4pt] f(-1) = -1(-1)^2 + 1 - 3 = -3 \end{aligned} $$ **d)** $$ \begin{aligned} f(-2) = -1(-2)^2 + 4 - 2 = -2 \\[4pt] f(-1) = -1(-1)^2 + 2 - 2 = -1 \\[4pt] f(0) = -1(0)^2 + 0 - 2 = -2 \end{aligned} $$ ::: ::: exercise-meta
Quadratic functions Form conversions Vertex form
::: ::::: ::::: {#exr-u7-26} *State the equation of the axis of symmetry.* :::: {.grid} ::: {.g-col-4} **a)** $\displaystyle f(x) = -x^2 - 4x - 4$ ::: ::: {.g-col-4} **b)** $\displaystyle f(x) = 2x^2 - 20x + 55$ ::: ::: {.g-col-4} **c)** $\displaystyle f(x) = -x^2 + 8x - 13$ ::: ::: {.g-col-4} **d)** $\displaystyle f(x) = x^2 - 2x + 3$ ::: ::: {.g-col-4} **e)** $\displaystyle f(x) = -2x^2 - 4x - 4$ ::: ::: {.g-col-4} **f)** $\displaystyle f(x) = -x^2 + 2x + 5$ ::: :::: ::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"} ## 🔍 View Solutions **a)** $$ \begin{aligned} x = \dfrac{-b}{2a} = \dfrac{-(-4)}{2(-1)} = -2 \end{aligned} $$ **b)** $$ \begin{aligned} x = \dfrac{-b}{2a} = \dfrac{-(-20)}{2(2)} = 5 \end{aligned} $$ **c)** $$ \begin{aligned} x = \dfrac{-b}{2a} = \dfrac{-(8)}{2(-1)} = 4 \end{aligned} $$ **d)** $$ \begin{aligned} x = \dfrac{-b}{2a} = \dfrac{-(-2)}{2(1)} = 1 \end{aligned} $$ **e)** $$ \begin{aligned} x = \dfrac{-b}{2a} = \dfrac{-(-4)}{2(-2)} = -1 \end{aligned} $$ **f)** $$ \begin{aligned} x = \dfrac{-b}{2a} = \dfrac{-(2)}{2(-1)} = 1 \end{aligned} $$ ::: ::: exercise-meta
Vertex Axis of symmetry Roots
::: ::::: ::::: {#exr-u7-34} *State whether each graph represents a function. Write Yes or No.* :::: {.grid} ::: {.g-col-6} **a)** ::: ::: {.g-col-6} **b)** ::: ::: {.g-col-6} **c)** ::: ::: {.g-col-6} **d)** ::: ::::: ::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"} ## 🔍 View Solutions ✅ **(a)** **Yes** — passes the Vertical Line Test ❌ **(b)** **No** — fails the Vertical Line Test ❌ **(c)** **No** — fails the Vertical Line Test ✅ **(d)** **Yes** — passes the Vertical Line Test ::: ::: exercise-meta
Function definition Domain & range Arrow diagrams
::: ::::: ::: math-sep ∑ × π ÷ √ ◆ ∞ ± ∫ ≠ Δ ::: ### 🟡 Medium ::::: {#exr-u7-9} *Read and interpret the linear function.* A phone plan costs $30 per month plus $3 per GB of data. **(a)** Write an equation for $T$ in terms of $g$. **(b)** Interpret the slope and intercept in context. **(c)** Find the value when $g = 5$. ::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"} ## 🔍 View Solution $$ \begin{aligned} \text{(a) } T = 3g + 30 \\[4pt] \text{(b) Slope } = 3: \text{ cost per GB used} \\[4pt] \text{Intercept } = 30: \text{ monthly base cost} \\[4pt] \text{(c) When } g = 5: T = 3(5) + 30 = 45 \text{ \$} \end{aligned} $$ ::: ::: exercise-meta
Linear functions Slope y-intercept
::: ::::: ::::: {#exr-u7-12} *Interpret the slope in context.* A plumber charges a call-out fee of $39 plus $2 per hour. The equation is $C = 2h + 39$. **(a)** Identify the slope and interpret it in context. **(b)** What does the intercept represent? ::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"} ## 🔍 View Solution $$ \begin{aligned} \text{(a) Slope } = 2 \text{ (C per hour)} \\[4pt] \text{cost per hour of work} \\[4pt] \text{(b) Intercept } = 39: \text{ fixed call-out fee} \end{aligned} $$ ::: ::: exercise-meta
Rate of change Slope formula Interpretation
::: ::::: ::::: {#exr-u7-13} *Find intercepts and analyse the graph.* Consider the linear function $y = -2x + 1$. **(a)** Find the $x$-intercept and $y$-intercept. **(b)** Use the graph to verify your intercepts. ::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"} ## 🔍 View Solution $$ \begin{aligned} y\text{-int: set }x=0: y = 1 \\[4pt] x\text{-int: set }y=0: 0 = -2x + 1 \Rightarrow x = \dfrac{1}{2} \\[4pt] \text{Plot the points: x-int at }(\dfrac{1}{2},\, 0)\text{ and y-int at }(0,\ 1). \end{aligned} $$ ::: ::: exercise-meta
Graphing Intercepts Parallel & perpendicular
::: ::::: ::::: {#exr-u7-14} *Write the equation of the line.* A line passes through $(1,\ -7)$ and $(0,\ -5)$. **(a)** Calculate the slope of the line. **(b)** Write the equation in the form $y = mx + c$. ::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"} ## 🔍 View Solution $$ \begin{aligned} m = \dfrac{-5 - (-7)}{0 - (1)} = -2 \\[4pt] y - (-7) = -2(x - (1)) \\[4pt] y = -2x - 5 \end{aligned} $$ ::: ::: exercise-meta
Graphing Intercepts Parallel & perpendicular
::: ::::: ::::: {#exr-u7-20} *Analyse the parabola.* The parabola is given by $f(x) = 2(x - 3)^2 - 1$. **(a)** State the vertex and axis of symmetry. **(b)** Does the parabola open upward or downward? Why? **(c)** Write the equation in standard form $f(x) = ax^2 + bx + c$. ::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"} ## 🔍 View Solution $$ \begin{aligned} \text{(a) Vertex: } (3,\ -1), \text{ axis: } x = 3 \\[4pt] \text{(b) Opens upward since } a = 2 \text{{ > 0}} \\[4pt] \text{(c) Standard form: } f(x) = 2x^2 - 12x + 17 \end{aligned} $$ ::: ::: exercise-meta
Quadratic functions Form conversions Vertex form
::: ::::: ::::: {#exr-u7-21} *Convert from vertex form to standard form.* :::: {.grid} ::: {.g-col-12} **a)** $\displaystyle f(x) = (x + 2)^2 + 6$ ::: ::: {.g-col-12} **b)** $\displaystyle f(x) = -2(x - 2)^2 - 6$ ::: ::: {.g-col-12} **c)** $\displaystyle f(x) = 2(x + 1)^2 - 3$ ::: ::: {.g-col-12} **d)** $\displaystyle f(x) = 2(x - 1)^2 - 2$ ::: :::: ::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"} ## 🔍 View Solutions **a)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Expand the bracket:} \\[4pt] = 1(x^2 + 4x + 4) + 6 \\[4pt] = x^2 + 4x + 10 \end{aligned} $$ **b)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Expand the bracket:} \\[4pt] = -2(x^2 - 4x + 4) - 6 \\[4pt] = -2x^2 + 8x - 14 \end{aligned} $$ **c)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Expand the bracket:} \\[4pt] = 2(x^2 + 2x + 1) - 3 \\[4pt] = 2x^2 + 4x - 1 \end{aligned} $$ **d)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Expand the bracket:} \\[4pt] = 2(x^2 - 2x + 1) - 2 \\[4pt] = 2x^2 - 4x \end{aligned} $$ ::: ::: exercise-meta
Quadratic functions Form conversions Vertex form
::: ::::: ::::: {#exr-u7-24} *Expand to standard form.* :::: {.grid} ::: {.g-col-12} **a)** $\displaystyle f(x) = (x - 1)(x - 5)$ ::: ::: {.g-col-12} **b)** $\displaystyle f(x) = -(x - 1)(x - 5)$ ::: ::: {.g-col-12} **c)** $\displaystyle f(x) = (x + 1)(x + 4)$ ::: ::: {.g-col-12} **d)** $\displaystyle f(x) = 2(x + 5)(x - 4)$ ::: :::: ::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"} ## 🔍 View Solutions **a)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Expand} \\[4pt] = 1(x^2 - 6x + 5) \\[4pt] = x^2 - 6x + 5 \end{aligned} $$ **b)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Expand} \\[4pt] = -1(x^2 - 6x + 5) \\[4pt] = -x^2 + 6x - 5 \end{aligned} $$ **c)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Expand} \\[4pt] = 1(x^2 + 5x + 4) \\[4pt] = x^2 + 5x + 4 \end{aligned} $$ **d)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Expand} \\[4pt] = 2(x^2 + x - 20) \\[4pt] = 2x^2 + 2x - 40 \end{aligned} $$ ::: ::: exercise-meta
Quadratic functions Form conversions Vertex form
::: ::::: ::::: {#exr-u7-25} *Find the vertex of each quadratic function.* :::: {.grid} ::: {.g-col-4} **a)** $\displaystyle f(x) = 3x^2 + 6x + 2$ ::: ::: {.g-col-4} **b)** $\displaystyle f(x) = 2x^2 + 12x + 22$ ::: ::: {.g-col-4} **c)** $\displaystyle f(x) = x^2 - 2x + 5$ ::: ::: {.g-col-4} **d)** $\displaystyle f(x) = -2x^2 + 2$ ::: ::: {.g-col-4} **e)** $\displaystyle f(x) = x^2 - 4x + 3$ ::: ::: {.g-col-4} **f)** $\displaystyle f(x) = 2x^2 + 2$ ::: :::: ::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"} ## 🔍 View Solutions **a)** $$ \begin{aligned} x_{\text{vertex}} = \dfrac{-b}{2a} = \dfrac{-(6)}{2(3)} = -1 \\[4pt] y = 3(-1)^2 - 6 + 2 = -1 \\[4pt] \text{Vertex: }\ x=-1,\ y=-1 \end{aligned} $$ **b)** $$ \begin{aligned} x_{\text{vertex}} = \dfrac{-b}{2a} = \dfrac{-(12)}{2(2)} = -3 \\[4pt] y = 2(-3)^2 - 36 + 22 = 4 \\[4pt] \text{Vertex: }\ x=-3,\ y=4 \end{aligned} $$ **c)** $$ \begin{aligned} x_{\text{vertex}} = \dfrac{-b}{2a} = \dfrac{-(-2)}{2(1)} = 1 \\[4pt] y = 1(1)^2 - 2 + 5 = 4 \\[4pt] \text{Vertex: }\ x=1,\ y=4 \end{aligned} $$ **d)** $$ \begin{aligned} x_{\text{vertex}} = \dfrac{-b}{2a} = \dfrac{-(0)}{2(-2)} = 0 \\[4pt] y = -2(0)^2 + 2 = 2 \\[4pt] \text{Vertex: }\ x=0,\ y=2 \end{aligned} $$ **e)** $$ \begin{aligned} x_{\text{vertex}} = \dfrac{-b}{2a} = \dfrac{-(-4)}{2(1)} = 2 \\[4pt] y = 1(2)^2 - 8 + 3 = -1 \\[4pt] \text{Vertex: }\ x=2,\ y=-1 \end{aligned} $$ **f)** $$ \begin{aligned} x_{\text{vertex}} = \dfrac{-b}{2a} = \dfrac{-(0)}{2(2)} = 0 \\[4pt] y = 2(0)^2 + 2 = 2 \\[4pt] \text{Vertex: }\ x=0,\ y=2 \end{aligned} $$ ::: ::: exercise-meta
Vertex Axis of symmetry Roots
::: ::::: ::::: {#exr-u7-27} *Read key features from the graph.* The graph of a quadratic function is shown. **(a)** State the roots of the function. **(b)** State the coordinates of the vertex. **(c)** Write the equation of the axis of symmetry. ::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"} ## 🔍 View Solution $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Roots: } x = 0 \text{ and } x = 4 \\[4pt] \text{Vertex: } (2,\ -4) \\[4pt] \text{Axis of symmetry: } x = 2 \end{aligned} $$ ::: ::: exercise-meta
Vertex Axis of symmetry Roots
::: ::::: ::::: {#exr-u7-28} *State the domain and range of each quadratic function.* :::: {.grid} ::: {.g-col-12} **a)** $\displaystyle f(x) = 2(x - 3)^2 + 2$ ::: ::: {.g-col-12} **b)** $\displaystyle f(x) = -(x - 4)^2 + 2$ ::: ::: {.g-col-12} **c)** $\displaystyle f(x) = -(x + 3)^2 - 1$ ::: ::: {.g-col-12} **d)** $\displaystyle f(x) = (x + 3)^2 - 2$ ::: :::: ::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"} ## 🔍 View Solutions **a)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Domain: all real numbers} \\[4pt] x \in \mathbb{R} \\[4pt] \text{Parabola opens upward; vertex at }(3,\ 2) \\[4pt] \text{Range: } y \geq 2 \end{aligned} $$ **b)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Domain: all real numbers} \\[4pt] x \in \mathbb{R} \\[4pt] \text{Parabola opens downward; vertex at }(4,\ 2) \\[4pt] \text{Range: } y \leq 2 \end{aligned} $$ **c)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Domain: all real numbers} \\[4pt] x \in \mathbb{R} \\[4pt] \text{Parabola opens downward; vertex at }(-3,\ -1) \\[4pt] \text{Range: } y \leq -1 \end{aligned} $$ **d)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Domain: all real numbers} \\[4pt] x \in \mathbb{R} \\[4pt] \text{Parabola opens upward; vertex at }(-3,\ -2) \\[4pt] \text{Range: } y \geq -2 \end{aligned} $$ ::: ::: exercise-meta
Domain & range Vertex Quadratic functions
::: ::::: ::::: {#exr-u7-29} *State the domain and range from the graph.* The graph of a quadratic function is shown. **(a)** State the domain of the function. **(b)** State the range of the function. ::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"} ## 🔍 View Solution $$ \begin{aligned} \text{(a) Domain: } x \in \mathbb{R} \\[4pt] \text{(b) Vertex at }(-2,\ -1)\text{; opens upward} \\[4pt] \text{Range: } y \geq -1 \end{aligned} $$ ::: ::: exercise-meta
Domain & range Vertex Quadratic functions
::: ::::: ::::: {#exr-u7-32} *Read the graph and find the equation.* The graph of a quadratic function is shown. **(a)** State the roots (x-intercepts) of the function. **(b)** State the coordinates of the vertex. **(c)** Write the equation of the axis of symmetry. **(d)** Write the equation of the quadratic function. ::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"} ## 🔍 View Solution $$ \begin{aligned} \text{(a) Roots: } x = -1 \text{ and } x = 1 \\[4pt] \text{(b) Vertex: } (0,\ -2) \\[4pt] \text{(c) Axis of symmetry: } x = 0 \\[4pt] \text{(d) Equation: } 2(x + 1)(x - 1) \text{ or } 2x^2 - 2 \end{aligned} $$ ::: ::: exercise-meta
Vertex Axis of symmetry Roots
::: ::::: ::: math-sep ∑ × π ÷ √ ◆ ∞ ± ∫ ≠ Δ ::: ### 🔴 Challenging ::::: {#exr-u7-15} *Find the equation of each line described.* :::: {.grid} ::: {.g-col-12} **a)** $\displaystyle \text{Parallel to } y = 3x + 3\text{, through } (-2,\ -3)$ ::: ::: {.g-col-12} **b)** $\displaystyle \text{Parallel to } y = 3x - 4\text{, through } (3,\ -2)$ ::: ::: {.g-col-12} **c)** $\displaystyle \text{Parallel to } y = -2x + 4\text{, through } (-3,\ 2)$ ::: ::: {.g-col-12} **d)** $\displaystyle \text{Parallel to } y = -x + 1\text{, through } (-3,\ 1)$ ::: :::: ::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"} ## 🔍 View Solutions **a)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Parallel lines have equal slopes: } m = 3 \\[4pt] \text{Through }(-2,\ -3): -3 = 3(-2) + c \\[4pt] c = 3 \\[4pt] y = 3x + 3 \end{aligned} $$ **b)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Parallel lines have equal slopes: } m = 3 \\[4pt] \text{Through }(3,\ -2): -2 = 3(3) + c \\[4pt] c = -11 \\[4pt] y = 3x - 11 \end{aligned} $$ **c)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Parallel lines have equal slopes: } m = -2 \\[4pt] \text{Through }(-3,\ 2): 2 = -2(-3) + c \\[4pt] c = -4 \\[4pt] y = -2x - 4 \end{aligned} $$ **d)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Parallel lines have equal slopes: } m = -1 \\[4pt] \text{Through }(-3,\ 1): 1 = -1(-3) + c \\[4pt] c = -2 \\[4pt] y = -x - 2 \end{aligned} $$ ::: ::: exercise-meta
Graphing Intercepts Parallel & perpendicular
::: ::::: ::::: {#exr-u7-16} *Find the equation of each line described.* :::: {.grid} ::: {.g-col-12} **a)** $\displaystyle \text{Perpendicular to } y = -x - 4\text{, through } (2,\ -2)$ ::: ::: {.g-col-12} **b)** $\displaystyle \text{Perpendicular to } y = 2x + 4\text{, through } (0,\ 0)$ ::: ::: {.g-col-12} **c)** $\displaystyle \text{Perpendicular to } y = -x + 1\text{, through } (-1,\ 0)$ ::: ::: {.g-col-12} **d)** $\displaystyle \text{Perpendicular to } y = 2x - 2\text{, through } (0,\ 1)$ ::: :::: ::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"} ## 🔍 View Solutions **a)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Perpendicular slope: } m_\perp = \dfrac{-1}{-1} = 1 \\[4pt] \text{Through }(2,\ -2): -2 = 1(2) + c \\[4pt] c = -4 \\[4pt] y = x - 4 \end{aligned} $$ **b)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Perpendicular slope: } m_\perp = \dfrac{-1}{2} = -\dfrac{1}{2} \\[4pt] \text{Through }(0,\ 0): 0 = -\dfrac{1}{2}(0) + c \\[4pt] c = 0 \\[4pt] y = -\dfrac{1}{2}x \end{aligned} $$ **c)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Perpendicular slope: } m_\perp = \dfrac{-1}{-1} = 1 \\[4pt] \text{Through }(-1,\ 0): 0 = 1(-1) + c \\[4pt] c = 1 \\[4pt] y = x + 1 \end{aligned} $$ **d)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Perpendicular slope: } m_\perp = \dfrac{-1}{2} = -\dfrac{1}{2} \\[4pt] \text{Through }(0,\ 1): 1 = -\dfrac{1}{2}(0) + c \\[4pt] c = 1 \\[4pt] y = -\dfrac{1}{2}x + 1 \end{aligned} $$ ::: ::: exercise-meta
Graphing Intercepts Parallel & perpendicular
::: ::::: ::::: {#exr-u7-17} *Form and solve a linear model.* A phone plan charges $10 per month plus $2 per GB of data. **(a)** Write an equation for total monthly bill $T$ after using $g$ GB. **(b)** Find the bill for 12 GB usage. **(c)** How many GB can be used for $90? ::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"} ## 🔍 View Solution $$ \begin{aligned} \text{(a) } T = 2g + 10 \\[4pt] \text{(b) } T = 2(12) + 10 = 34 \text{ \$} \\[4pt] \text{(c) } 90 = 2g + 10 \Rightarrow g = 40 \text{ GB} \end{aligned} $$ ::: ::: exercise-meta
Linear modelling Real-world context Interpretation
::: ::::: ::::: {#exr-u7-22} *Convert from vertex form to factored form.* :::: {.grid} ::: {.g-col-12} **a)** $\displaystyle f(x) = (x + 1)^2 - 1$ ::: ::: {.g-col-12} **b)** $\displaystyle f(x) = (x - 2)^2 - 4$ ::: ::: {.g-col-12} **c)** $\displaystyle f(x) = (x + 3)^2 - 4$ ::: ::: {.g-col-12} **d)** $\displaystyle f(x) = (x - 4)^2 - 4$ ::: :::: ::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"} ## 🔍 View Solutions **a)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Set } f(x) = 0: \\[4pt] (x - (-1))^2 = 1 \\[4pt] x + 1 = \pm 1 \\[4pt] x = -2 \text{ or } x = 0 \\[4pt] \therefore f(x) = (x + 2)(x) \end{aligned} $$ **b)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Set } f(x) = 0: \\[4pt] (x - (2))^2 = 4 \\[4pt] x - 2 = \pm 2 \\[4pt] x = 0 \text{ or } x = 4 \\[4pt] \therefore f(x) = (x)(x - 4) \end{aligned} $$ **c)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Set } f(x) = 0: \\[4pt] (x - (-3))^2 = 4 \\[4pt] x + 3 = \pm 2 \\[4pt] x = -5 \text{ or } x = -1 \\[4pt] \therefore f(x) = (x + 5)(x + 1) \end{aligned} $$ **d)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Set } f(x) = 0: \\[4pt] (x - (4))^2 = 4 \\[4pt] x - 4 = \pm 2 \\[4pt] x = 2 \text{ or } x = 6 \\[4pt] \therefore f(x) = (x - 2)(x - 6) \end{aligned} $$ ::: ::: exercise-meta
Quadratic functions Form conversions Vertex form
::: ::::: ::::: {#exr-u7-23} *Convert to vertex form by completing the square.* :::: {.grid} ::: {.g-col-12} **a)** $\displaystyle f(x) = -x^2 + 2x - 7$ ::: ::: {.g-col-12} **b)** $\displaystyle f(x) = x^2 - 4$ ::: ::: {.g-col-12} **c)** $\displaystyle f(x) = -x^2 - 6$ ::: ::: {.g-col-12} **d)** $\displaystyle f(x) = x^2 - 4x + 3$ ::: :::: ::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"} ## 🔍 View Solutions **a)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Factor out } -1\text{:} \\[4pt] = -1\left(x^2 - 2x\right) - 7 \\[4pt] = -1\left(x - 1\right)^2 + 1 - 7 \\[4pt] = -(x - 1)^2 - 6 \end{aligned} $$ **b)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Complete the square:} \\[4pt] = (x + 0)^2 - 0 - 4 \\[4pt] = (x + 0)^2 - 4 \\[4pt] = (x)^2 - 4 \end{aligned} $$ **c)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Factor out } -1\text{:} \\[4pt] = -1\left(x^2 + 0x\right) - 6 \\[4pt] = -1\left(x + 0\right)^2 + 0 - 6 \\[4pt] = -(x)^2 - 6 \end{aligned} $$ **d)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Complete the square:} \\[4pt] = (x - 2)^2 - 4 + 3 \\[4pt] = (x - 2)^2 - 1 \\[4pt] = (x - 2)^2 - 1 \end{aligned} $$ ::: ::: exercise-meta
Quadratic functions Form conversions Vertex form
::: ::::: ::::: {#exr-u7-30} *Analyse the projectile model.* A ball is thrown upward. Its height (m) after $t$ seconds is $h(t) = -5t^2 + 15t$. **(a)** What is the initial height of the ball? **(b)** Find the maximum height and when it occurs. **(c)** Set up the equation to find when the ball hits the ground. ::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"} ## 🔍 View Solution $$ \begin{aligned} \text{(a) } h(0) = 0 \text{ m — initial height} \\[4pt] \text{(b) Vertex: } t = \dfrac{-15}{2(-5)} = \dfrac{3}{2} \text{ s} \\[4pt] \text{Max height } = 11 \text{ m} \\[4pt] \text{(c) Set } h = 0: -5t^2 + 15t = 0 \end{aligned} $$ ::: ::: exercise-meta
Quadratic modelling Projectile motion Revenue
::: ::::: ::::: {#exr-u7-31} *Analyse the revenue model.* A company sells items at price $p$ dollars. Demand is $100 - 2p$ units. **(a)** Write an equation for revenue $R$ in terms of $p$. **(b)** Find the price that maximises revenue and state the maximum revenue. ::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"} ## 🔍 View Solution $$ \begin{aligned} \text{(a) Demand } = 100 - 2p \\[4pt] R = p(100 - 2p) = 100p - 2p^2 \\[4pt] \text{(b) Vertex: } p = \dfrac{100}{2 \times 2} = 25 \\[4pt] R_{\text{max}} = 1250 \end{aligned} $$ ::: ::: exercise-meta
Quadratic modelling Projectile motion Revenue
::: ::::: ::::: {#exr-u7-33} *Find the intersection of the two functions.* $f(x) = x^2 - 9x + 6$ and $g(x) = -2x - 4$ **(a)** Set $f(x) = g(x)$ and form a quadratic equation. **(b)** Solve to find the $x$-coordinates of the intersection points. **(c)** State the coordinates of the intersection points. ::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"} ## 🔍 View Solution $$ \begin{aligned} \textbf{(a)} \quad x^2 - 9x + 6 = -2x - 4 \implies x^2 - 7x + 10 = 0 \\[6pt] \textbf{(b)} \quad (x - 2)(x - 5) = 0 \implies x = 2 \text{ or } x = 5 \\[6pt] \textbf{(c)} \quad (2,\ -8) \text{ and } (5,\ -14) \end{aligned} $$ ::: ::: exercise-meta
